narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your dream of becoming a lawyer, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to passing the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 47% chance of failure that tipped the scales against your application. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 3 decile of 1.0, which stood out as the biggest red flag, whispering that even if you started stronger in Year 1 with a solid decile of 4.0, your performance dipped sharply later on, painting a picture of struggles mounting over time. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 27.0, which the model saw as a hurdle too high for the demands of bar prep, pulling strongly toward that tougher outcome. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 added to the concern, suggesting the foundational academic rigor might not fully carry you through. Then there were the broader strokes—your Native American background and lower-middle family income, which the model linked to extra barriers others might not face, like fewer resources or support networks that often make the difference. Choosing part-time status while studying tugged in the same direction, hinting at divided focus that could dilute your bar prep. Even as a female applicant with clear ambitions for law school, these threads wove together into a narrative the model couldn't ignore, one where the risks outweighed the promise. It's not that your full story isn't inspiring—far from it—but this combination just echoed patterns we've seen lead to heartbreak before. The good news? You're not defined by one prediction; with targeted boosts like LSAT retakes or stronger late-year grades, you could rewrite this ending. Keep pushing—you've got the heart for this.
